content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I remember the first time I tried to learn coding.
I Googled “how to learn programming,” clicked the first result, and landed on a page full of terms I didn’t understand. Variables. Functions. Loops. Syntax errors. It felt like someone handed me a book written in a language I’d never seen before and said, “Good luck.”
I closed the tab. Told myself coding was for “technical people.” And moved on.
Maybe you’ve done the same thing. Maybe you’re doing it right now — reading this with one part of your brain curious and another part already making excuses.
Here’s what I want to tell you: coding is not for a special type of person. It’s a skill. Like driving. Like cooking. You learn it by doing it, not by being born with it.
Let me show you how to actually start — without the overwhelm.
First, Stop Asking “Which Language Should I Learn?”
This is the question that paralyzes almost every beginner.
Python or JavaScript? HTML or C++? Should I learn what’s trending or what pays the most?
Here’s the honest answer: it doesn’t matter as much as you think.
Your first language is not your forever language. It’s just the language you use to understand how coding works — how computers think, how instructions flow, how problems get broken down into steps.
For most beginners, Python is the gentlest starting point. It reads almost like plain English, the syntax is clean, and there’s more beginner learning material for Python than almost anything else. In the US and UK, Python is widely used in data, automation, and backend development. In Pakistan, it’s also one of the most in-demand skills on freelancing platforms like Upwork and Fiverr.
If your goal is building websites or getting into freelancing quickly, start with HTML and CSS first — they’re not technically “programming languages” but they’ll teach you how the web works and give you visible results fast.
Pick one. Start. Switch later if you need to.
The Mistake That Kills Most Beginners
They watch tutorials for weeks and never write a single line of code themselves.
There’s a name for this: tutorial hell. You watch a YouTube video, it makes sense, you feel productive, you watch another one. Then another. Then one day you open a blank file to write something yourself and your mind goes completely empty.
That feeling? That’s not stupidity. That’s what happens when you consume without practicing.
Coding is a physical skill as much as a mental one. Your fingers need to learn the patterns. Your brain needs to make mistakes and fix them. You cannot learn it by watching, the same way you cannot learn to swim by watching swimming videos.
The rule that changed everything for me: for every 20 minutes of tutorial, write code for 40 minutes. Even if it’s broken. Even if you’re just copying what you saw and changing small things. Type it yourself. Don’t copy-paste.
You Don’t Need an Expensive Course or a Degree
This is important — especially for readers in countries where paying for courses in dollars feels impossible.
There are genuinely free resources that are as good as anything paid. FreeCodeCamp teaches full web development completely free, and their certificates are recognized by real employers. The Odin Project is another free full-stack curriculum that takes you from zero to job-ready. CS50 — Harvard’s intro to computer science — is available on edX for free and is one of the best beginner courses ever made.
In Pakistan and South Asia, many beginners worry that without a computer science degree, no one will take them seriously. That’s becoming less true every year. Companies in the US, UK, Canada, and increasingly in Pakistan itself are hiring based on what you can build — not where you studied.
A GitHub profile with real projects tells an employer more than a certificate ever will.
What Your First 30 Days Should Actually Look Like
Week 1: Pick Python or HTML/CSS. Go to FreeCodeCamp or follow CS50. Spend 30–45 minutes a day. Just learn the basics — what variables are, how to write simple instructions, how to see output on your screen.
Week 2: Start a tiny project. Doesn’t matter what. A simple calculator. A webpage about your favorite topic. A program that tells you if a number is odd or even. Tiny and real beats big and imaginary every time.
Week 3: Break things on purpose. Change something in your code and see what happens. Read the error message. Google it. Fix it. This process — write, break, Google, fix — is literally what professional developers do every single day.
Week 4: Show someone what you built. Post it online. Share it in a beginner community. The act of showing your work forces you to think more clearly about what you’ve made — and beginner communities are some of the kindest places on the internet.
The Loneliest Part Nobody Warns You About
There will be a day — probably around week two or three — where something won’t work and you won’t know why.
You’ll read the code a hundred times. It’ll look fine. But it won’t run. Or it’ll run and do something completely wrong.
This moment is where most beginners quit. They take it personally. They think it means they’re not cut out for this.
But here’s what’s actually happening: you’re programming. This is what it feels like. Professional developers with ten years of experience sit with broken code for hours. Debugging — finding and fixing errors — is not a sign that you’re failing. It’s the job.
The only difference between a beginner and an experienced developer is that the experienced developer has been confused more times and pushed through more often.
One Thing to Remember
You don’t need to learn everything before you start.
You don’t need the best laptop. You don’t need a fast internet connection every day. You don’t need to live in a tech hub. Developers have built careers from small towns in Pakistan, Nigeria, Romania, and everywhere else you’d never think to look for a Silicon Valley success story.
What you need is 30 minutes a day, a free resource, and the willingness to feel confused without quitting.
The first line of code you write will be bad. Write it anyway.
The first project you build will be small. Build it anyway.
Because every developer you admire — every person building apps, landing remote jobs, freelancing for international clients — started exactly where you are right now.
They just didn’t stop.
